text: This video demonstrates early transanal closure after vacuum assisted drainage for anastomatic leakage in rectal cancer surgery. This case involves a seventy-seven year old male patient presenting with a T-three-N-zero rectal tumor with a clear circumferential resection margin.

Patient did not receive neoadjuvant treatment and underwent a low anterior resection with a side-to-end anastomosis, six centimeters from the anal verge. Patient did not receive a diverting ileostomy.
On postoperative day four, the patient developed increasing abdominal pain with high infectious parameters. Abdominal CT scan showed an anastomatic leak from the circular anastomosis.
The patient was taken to the operating theater for a relaparoscopy and creation of a diverting ileostomy. Endosponge treatment was started to control pelvic sepsis.
Endosponges were changed every three to four days. Usually it only takes two to four endosponge changes to obtain a clean cavity, but this case took particularly long.

Two months post-operation, the presacral cavity is observed to be clean with granulation tissue. The surgical team prepares for closure by installing the transanal platform under general anesthesia following a perianal block.
The surgeon visualizes the blind loop, afferent loop, and anastomotic defect. A piece of gauze is inserted into the afferent loop to prevent accidental closure during subsequent transanal suturing.
The surgeon inspects a clean granulating cavity, noting increased fibrosis due to multiple endosponge changes. Mobilization of the rectal cuff from surrounding structures is required.
The surgeon places a six to eight French suction drain into the old abscess cavity before closing the defect, aiming to prevent the formation of a new abscess.

The surgeon inserts a suction drain one to two centimeters distal to the anastomotic defect, positioning it immediately posterior to the rectal wall. The drain is then secured using a fast-resorbable Ficryl-III-Zero suture.

The transanal platform is removed and repositioned to keep the drain outside its lumen, minimizing interference during suture placement. The drain is visibly fixated outside the lumen of the transanal platform.

The surgeon closes the cavity using separate interrupted full thickness Ficryl-II-Zero sutures. Gas is visible in the afferent loop, preventing its complete closure during the suturing process. After the defect is fully closed, the gas will be removed to ensure the afferent loop remains patent.

Standard postoperative protocol entails drain removal on postoperative day five to seven and oral antibiotics for seven to ten days. Anastomotic integrity is checked by an endoscopy and a CT scan with rectal contrast. If both show a healed anastomosis, the diverting ileostomy can be closed.
This video demonstrates our technique for approximation of the anastomotic defect after vacuum assisted drainage. Thank you for watching.
